title: Contextuality
definition: Adapt the application of regenerative practices to the territorial, environmental, social and agronomic context of each farm, in coherence with long-term planning.
detail: This includes management practices adapted to the farm, soil, climate and water availability, while also increasing resilience to the climate emergency. Furthermore, it involves integrating ancestral knowledge and local indigenous regenerative practices with newer ones and considering the social organisation of the territory (watersheds plans, protected areas, etc.), topography, farm size, flora and fauna, soil type, water availability, rainfall and also the family and neighbouring context. This criterion prioritizes ecological and productive coherence over the application of rigid models, promoting technical autonomy and informed decision-making on each farm.

title: Water
definition: Plan, size and develop agricultural and livestock production in accordance with water availability and the geological and edaphoclimatic reality of the basin, and carry out hydrological management practices that promote responsible and non-polluting use of water and prevent the over consumption of ground water levels.
detail: These practices must prevent the over-consumption of water and promote the natural recharge of the ground water, while optimizing irrigation according to agroclimatic conditions and the improvement of soil health. Practices that improve water retention and infiltration must be encouraged, as well as the capture and storage of rainwater within the landscape itself, through ponds, terraces, runoff retention systems following contour lines or off-the-contour patterns, and water harvesting and storage using natural methods.

title: Biodiversity
definition: Implement practices that actively protect and increase both wild and cultivated/domestic biodiversity.
detail: These practices include planned grazing; the promotion of natural pollinators, the installation of insect hotels, hedges and living borders; the integration of flower strips, ponds or wildlife refuges; the promotion of a wide range of plant species; ecological connections with the surrounding environment such as living corridors, the maintenance of margins and other natural elements that constitute a mosaic-like landscape; and the use of adapted, traditional and/or native breeds.

title: Soil Management
detail: No-Till: There should be no tillage that turns over soil layers, as soil disturbances negatively impact soil structure and biology. However, when in transition, topsoil layers can be tilled through minimal vertical tillage. This is because minimal vertical tillage can improve residue management and seedbed conditions without deep soil disturbances.

title: Soil Cover
definition: Keep the soil covered with plant or animal matter for as long as possible throughout the year within the hydrological availability and limitations of the basin and ensuring a positive carbon cycle limiting oxidation.
detail: This can be achieved with various strategies, such as cover crops, productive crops, meadows, spontaneous vegetation or mulching (organic mulch such as woodchips, shredded pruning, BRF, algae, wool…).

title: Crop Diversification
definition: In the case of seasonal crops, apply associations and/or rotations and/or crop diversification by combining them in space and time. In the case of perennial crops, apply intercropping and/or agroforestry and/or vegetation diversification.
detail: Among the temporal strategies are the rotation of legumes and pasture cropping. There should also be a focus on biological tillage, done through deep rooting plants that promote soil structure and porosity. Among the spatial ones, agroforestry and intensive no-till farming systems (such as market gardening, biointensive systems and syntropic agroforestry) are recommended.

title: Inputs
definition: Manage the farm without synthetic chemical fertilizers or biocides (herbicides, insecticides and fungicides) or extensive use of external inputs that exceed the limitations of the basin.
detail: Regenerative agriculture is based on protecting soil microbiology and promoting ecosystem processes as the foundation for the fertility, health, and resilience of the agroecosystem. It therefore prioritizes management systems that reduce the need for external inputs. Inputs are considered according to their impact, necessity and method of use, not solely on their origin. The goal is that, as the soil recovers its functionality, dependence on external inputs gradually decreases until they are no longer necessary.

title: Plastics & Waste
definition: Minimize the use of plastic and non-recyclable materials, and ensure proper recycling of those that are used.
detail: The use of single-use plastics (nets, trays, etc.) must be minimized, and the use of reusable, recycled and recyclable materials is encouraged, ensuring their responsible and traceable disposal, as well as the use of reusable, recycled and recyclable materials for agricultural management and packaging.

title: Social & Territorial Foundations
definition: Promote landscape-wide cooperation and knowledge transfer, to strengthen the community and the sustainability of territories.
detail: To meet this criterion, at least two of the following options must be practiced: offer guided tours or open days; be part of local collaborative networks; participate in knowledge exchange networks; actively participate in associations and community activities to transfer knowledge through public events and/or social media; collaborate with schools or research centres; integrate family and collaborators into learning processes; be in a process of continuous education; actively contribute to the collective care of the territory.

title: Economic Viability
definition: The farming practices should promote a minimal financial input by using on-farm inputs whilst the farmer should have market access to ensure their product can be sold through the shortest possible supply chain.
detail: The use of on-farm inputs should be promoted to minimize external inputs and thus financial burdens. Work with local markets in order to shorten supply chains; become part of a producer or consumer cooperative to offset product through direct supply chains.
